Project 1- checkpoint 3

Author

Addie Weaver, Scarlet Kwan, Sophia Woodward 

library(tidyverse)
library(gt)
library(glue)
library(httr)
library(tidyjson)
library(jsonlite)
democracy_data <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2024/2024-11-05/democracy_data.csv')

Data Sources

#1

api_key <- "bBv48CNNcVrUX1b43G8XxjlVQE1nYi4MMkM8OKfR" 
# link to this data set https://api-ninjas.com/api/gdp

get_gdp <- function(country, key = api_key)
  {
  
  url <- glue("https://api.api-ninjas.com/v1/gdp?country={country};X-Api-Key={api_key}")
  my_data <- safely(fromJSON)(url)
  
  res <- my_data$result
  
  #had to change it to length & null cause i kept getting one or the other
   if (is.null(res) || length(res) == 0) {
    return(tibble(
      country_code = character(),
      year = integer(),
      gdp = double()
    ))
  } else {
    return( #made a tibble for the 3 pieces of data I got from the api
      as_tibble(res) |>
        select(country, year, gdp_nominal) |>
        mutate(
          country_code = country,
          year = as.integer(year),
          gdp = as.numeric(gdp_nominal)
        ) |>
        select(country_code, year, gdp)
    )
  }
}

Data Joins Democracy & GDP For DataSet 1

#vector of the country_codes
# get unique country codes only
country_codes <- democracy_data |>
  distinct(country_code) |>
  pull(country_code)

# pull GDP data for all available countries
gdp_df <- country_codes |>
  map(get_gdp) |>
  bind_rows()

#gdp_df

democracy_gdp <- democracy_data |>
  mutate(
    female_leader = is_female_monarch == TRUE | is_female_president == TRUE
  ) |>
  left_join(gdp_df, by = c("country_code", "year"))

democracy_gdp
# A tibble: 14,828 × 45
   country_name country_code  year regime_category_index regime_category   
   <chr>        <chr>        <dbl>                 <dbl> <chr>             
 1 Afghanistan  AFG           1950                     5 Royal dictatorship
 2 Afghanistan  AFG           1951                     5 Royal dictatorship
 3 Afghanistan  AFG           1952                     5 Royal dictatorship
 4 Afghanistan  AFG           1953                     5 Royal dictatorship
 5 Afghanistan  AFG           1954                     5 Royal dictatorship
 6 Afghanistan  AFG           1955                     5 Royal dictatorship
 7 Afghanistan  AFG           1956                     5 Royal dictatorship
 8 Afghanistan  AFG           1957                     5 Royal dictatorship
 9 Afghanistan  AFG           1958                     5 Royal dictatorship
10 Afghanistan  AFG           1959                     5 Royal dictatorship
# ℹ 14,818 more rows
# ℹ 40 more variables: is_monarchy <lgl>, is_commonwealth <lgl>,
#   monarch_name <chr>, monarch_accession_year <dbl>, monarch_birthyear <dbl>,
#   is_female_monarch <lgl>, is_democracy <lgl>, is_presidential <lgl>,
#   president_name <chr>, president_accesion_year <dbl>,
#   president_birthyear <dbl>, is_interim_phase <lgl>,
#   is_female_president <lgl>, is_colony <lgl>, colony_of <chr>, …

The new data set we added is from a GDP API based on country and year. To add this to our overall data set we joined it by country and year. The variables added were GDP for each country.

What we plan to do with this data is look into which female led countries have the highest GDP.

#2

get_unemployment <- function(country, key = api_key)
{
  
  url <- glue("https://api.api-ninjas.com/v1/unemployment?country={country};X-Api-Key={key}")
  my_data <- safely(fromJSON)(url)
  
  res <- my_data$result
  
  if (is.null(res) || length(res) == 0) {
    return(tibble(
      country_code = character(),
      year = integer(),
      unemployment_rate = double()
    ))
  } else {
    return(
      as_tibble(res) |> #made a tibble using the 3 piences of data from api
        select(country, year, unemployment_rate) |>
        mutate(
          country_code = country,
          year = as.integer(year),
          unemployment_rate = as.numeric(unemployment_rate)
        ) |>
        select(country_code, year, unemployment_rate)
    )
  }
}

Data Joins Democracy & Unenployment For DataSet 2

country_codes <- democracy_data |>
  distinct(country_code) |>
  pull(country_code)


unemp_df <- country_codes |>
  map(get_unemployment) |>
  bind_rows()
democracy_unemp <- democracy_data |>
  inner_join(unemp_df, by = c("country_code", "year"))

democracy_unemp
# A tibble: 3,604 × 44
   country_name country_code  year regime_category_index regime_category      
   <chr>        <chr>        <dbl>                 <dbl> <chr>                
 1 Albania      ALB           1980                     4 Military dictatorship
 2 Albania      ALB           1981                     4 Military dictatorship
 3 Albania      ALB           1982                     4 Military dictatorship
 4 Albania      ALB           1983                     4 Military dictatorship
 5 Albania      ALB           1984                     4 Military dictatorship
 6 Albania      ALB           1985                     3 Civilian dictatorship
 7 Albania      ALB           1986                     3 Civilian dictatorship
 8 Albania      ALB           1987                     3 Civilian dictatorship
 9 Albania      ALB           1988                     3 Civilian dictatorship
10 Albania      ALB           1989                     3 Civilian dictatorship
# ℹ 3,594 more rows
# ℹ 39 more variables: is_monarchy <lgl>, is_commonwealth <lgl>,
#   monarch_name <chr>, monarch_accession_year <dbl>, monarch_birthyear <dbl>,
#   is_female_monarch <lgl>, is_democracy <lgl>, is_presidential <lgl>,
#   president_name <chr>, president_accesion_year <dbl>,
#   president_birthyear <dbl>, is_interim_phase <lgl>,
#   is_female_president <lgl>, is_colony <lgl>, colony_of <chr>, …

This new data includes our previously joined democracy and GDP data. We added an unemployment rate API data set and used joins to make a new data set, joining it by country and year. By doing so, we added the variable unemployment for each country and year, which we could now use to see if there is a relationship between unemployment rate and other variables in our previously combined data set. We chose to see if the type of regime a country/region has an effect on their unemployment rate.

Data Summaries

Research Question: GDP For Top Countries With Most Female Leaders Over Time

top_10_female_leaders_gdp <- democracy_gdp |>
  filter(!is.na(gdp)) |>
  group_by(country_name, country_code) |>
  summarize(
    avg_gdp = mean(gdp),
    n_female_leaders = sum(female_leader, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  slice_max(n_female_leaders, n = 10, with_ties = FALSE)

top_10_female_leaders_gdp
# A tibble: 10 × 4
   country_name   country_code avg_gdp n_female_leaders
   <chr>          <chr>          <dbl>            <int>
 1 Denmark        DNK           211.                 41
 2 United Kingdom GBR          1814.                 41
 3 Netherlands    NLD           541.                 33
 4 Ireland        IRL           153.                 21
 5 Aruba          ABW             2.39               18
 6 Hong Kong      HKG           170.                 18
 7 Iceland        ISL            11.4                16
 8 Liberia        LBR             2.06               15
 9 Philippines    PHL           137.                 15
10 Chile          CHL           120.                 12
#Table of Top Female Leaders with their GDP
gt::gt(top_10_female_leaders_gdp) |>
  cols_label(
  country_name = "Country Name",
  country_code = "Country Code",
  avg_gdp = "Average GDP",
  n_female_leaders ="Number of Female Leaders")|>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
    ) |> tab_header(
      title = md("Top Countries with Female Leaders Average GDP")
    )
Top Countries with Female Leaders Average GDP
Country Name Country Code Average GDP Number of Female Leaders
Denmark DNK 210.875390 41
United Kingdom GBR 1813.737220 41
Netherlands NLD 541.313293 33
Ireland IRL 152.633366 21
Aruba ABW 2.386615 18
Hong Kong HKG 169.760000 18
Iceland ISL 11.353293 16
Liberia LBR 2.063048 15
Philippines PHL 137.231293 15
Chile CHL 120.221293 12

#This plot is a Visualization of the information in the table above.

ggplot(top_10_female_leaders_gdp, aes(x = n_female_leaders, y = avg_gdp))+
  geom_point(position = position_jitter(width = 0.2, seed = 123))+
  geom_line(position = position_jitter(width = 0.2, seed = 123))+
  labs(title = "Top Countries with Female Leaders Average GDP", 
       x="Total Number of Female Leaders", 
       y= "Average GDP")+
  theme_minimal()

#2 Research Question: Does the type of regime of a country/region affect the unemployment rate?

unemploy_data <- democracy_unemp |>
  inner_join(gdp_df, by = c("country_code", "year"), relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  filter( year == "2019",
          !is.na(regime_category_index)) 

ggplot(unemploy_data,
       aes(x = regime_category, y = unemployment_rate)) +
  geom_boxplot() +  
  labs(title = "Political Regime Type and Unemployment Rate Across Countries (2019)",
       x = "Regime Category", 
       y = "Unemployment Rate") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

#The boxplot above shows us that the unemployment rate looks pretty similar among different regime categories, with similar medians and IQR.

```{r}
#| label: install-packages-and-read-in-data
#| message: false 

library(tidyverse)
library(gt)
library(glue)
library(httr)
library(tidyjson)
library(jsonlite)
democracy_data <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2024/2024-11-05/democracy_data.csv')

```

## Data Sources

### #1

```{r}
api_key <- "bBv48CNNcVrUX1b43G8XxjlVQE1nYi4MMkM8OKfR" 
# link to this data set https://api-ninjas.com/api/gdp

get_gdp <- function(country, key = api_key)
  {
  
  url <- glue("https://api.api-ninjas.com/v1/gdp?country={country};X-Api-Key={api_key}")
  my_data <- safely(fromJSON)(url)
  
  res <- my_data$result
  
  #had to change it to length & null cause i kept getting one or the other
   if (is.null(res) || length(res) == 0) {
    return(tibble(
      country_code = character(),
      year = integer(),
      gdp = double()
    ))
  } else {
    return( #made a tibble for the 3 pieces of data I got from the api
      as_tibble(res) |>
        select(country, year, gdp_nominal) |>
        mutate(
          country_code = country,
          year = as.integer(year),
          gdp = as.numeric(gdp_nominal)
        ) |>
        select(country_code, year, gdp)
    )
  }
}

```

## Data Joins Democracy & GDP For DataSet 1
```{r}
#vector of the country_codes
# get unique country codes only
country_codes <- democracy_data |>
  distinct(country_code) |>
  pull(country_code)

# pull GDP data for all available countries
gdp_df <- country_codes |>
  map(get_gdp) |>
  bind_rows()

#gdp_df

democracy_gdp <- democracy_data |>
  mutate(
    female_leader = is_female_monarch == TRUE | is_female_president == TRUE
  ) |>
  left_join(gdp_df, by = c("country_code", "year"))

democracy_gdp

```

The new data set we added is from a GDP API based on country and year. To add this to our overall data set we joined it by country and year. The variables added were GDP for each country.

What we plan to do with this data is look into which female led countries have the highest GDP.

### #2

```{r}
get_unemployment <- function(country, key = api_key)
{
  
  url <- glue("https://api.api-ninjas.com/v1/unemployment?country={country};X-Api-Key={key}")
  my_data <- safely(fromJSON)(url)
  
  res <- my_data$result
  
  if (is.null(res) || length(res) == 0) {
    return(tibble(
      country_code = character(),
      year = integer(),
      unemployment_rate = double()
    ))
  } else {
    return(
      as_tibble(res) |> #made a tibble using the 3 piences of data from api
        select(country, year, unemployment_rate) |>
        mutate(
          country_code = country,
          year = as.integer(year),
          unemployment_rate = as.numeric(unemployment_rate)
        ) |>
        select(country_code, year, unemployment_rate)
    )
  }
}

```
## Data Joins Democracy & Unenployment For DataSet 2
```{r}
#| label: mapping-unemployment-data

country_codes <- democracy_data |>
  distinct(country_code) |>
  pull(country_code)


unemp_df <- country_codes |>
  map(get_unemployment) |>
  bind_rows()
```


```{r}
#| label: join-enemployment-data-and-unemployment-data

democracy_unemp <- democracy_data |>
  inner_join(unemp_df, by = c("country_code", "year"))

democracy_unemp
```


This new data includes our previously joined democracy and GDP data. We added an unemployment rate API data set and used joins to make a new data set, joining it by country and year. By doing so, we added the variable unemployment for each country and year, which we could now use to see if there is a relationship between unemployment rate and other variables in our previously combined data set. We chose to see if the type of regime a country/region has an effect on their unemployment rate.

## Data Summaries

### Research Question: GDP For Top Countries With Most Female Leaders Over Time

```{r}
top_10_female_leaders_gdp <- democracy_gdp |>
  filter(!is.na(gdp)) |>
  group_by(country_name, country_code) |>
  summarize(
    avg_gdp = mean(gdp),
    n_female_leaders = sum(female_leader, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  slice_max(n_female_leaders, n = 10, with_ties = FALSE)

top_10_female_leaders_gdp
#Table of Top Female Leaders with their GDP
gt::gt(top_10_female_leaders_gdp) |>
  cols_label(
  country_name = "Country Name",
  country_code = "Country Code",
  avg_gdp = "Average GDP",
  n_female_leaders ="Number of Female Leaders")|>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
    ) |> tab_header(
      title = md("Top Countries with Female Leaders Average GDP")
    )

```

#This plot is a Visualization of the information in the table above.
```{r}
ggplot(top_10_female_leaders_gdp, aes(x = n_female_leaders, y = avg_gdp))+
  geom_point(position = position_jitter(width = 0.2, seed = 123))+
  geom_line(position = position_jitter(width = 0.2, seed = 123))+
  labs(title = "Top Countries with Female Leaders Average GDP", 
       x="Total Number of Female Leaders", 
       y= "Average GDP")+
  theme_minimal()
```

### #2 Research Question: Does the type of regime of a country/region affect the unemployment rate?
```{r}
#| label: visualization-to-answer-research question


unemploy_data <- democracy_unemp |>
  inner_join(gdp_df, by = c("country_code", "year"), relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  filter( year == "2019",
          !is.na(regime_category_index)) 

ggplot(unemploy_data,
       aes(x = regime_category, y = unemployment_rate)) +
  geom_boxplot() +  
  labs(title = "Political Regime Type and Unemployment Rate Across Countries (2019)",
       x = "Regime Category", 
       y = "Unemployment Rate") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

```
#The boxplot above shows us that the unemployment rate looks pretty similar among different regime categories, with similar medians and IQR.
